flashcards.front: What is a storage class in C?
flashcards.back: A storage class defines the scope (visibility), lifetime (duration), and memory location of a variable. It specifies how storage is allocated and deallocated for variables in a C program.

flashcards.front: What are the four storage classes in C?
flashcards.back: The four storage classes in C are: auto, register, static, and extern. Each determines different properties for variables regarding their storage duration and scope.

Auto (automatic) is the default storage class for local variables. Variables are created when the block is entered and destroyed when the block exits. They are stored in stack memory and have no linkage.
Register requests the compiler to store the variable in a CPU register instead of RAM for faster access. It cannot use the '&' operator (address-of). Has automatic storage duration and block scope.
Static variables retain their value between function calls. They have static storage duration (exist for entire program runtime) and can have internal or external linkage depending on placement.
Extern declares a variable defined elsewhere in the program (possibly in another file). It provides external linkage, allowing variables to be shared across multiple source files.
Storage duration determines when variable storage is allocated and deallocated. C has three: automatic (auto, register), static (static, extern), and allocated (dynamic memory).

flashcards.front: What is the difference between scope and linkage?
flashcards.back: Scope is where a variable can be accessed within the source code (block, function, file). Linkage determines whether the same identifier in different scopes refers to the same variable.

flashcards.front: Where is a static local variable stored?
flashcards.back: Static local variables are stored in the data segment of memory (not stack). They are initialized only once at program start and retain values between function calls.

flashcards.front: What is the default storage class for a local variable?
flashcards.back: The default storage class for local variables is 'auto' (automatic). They are created when the block enters and destroyed when the block exits.

flashcards.front: What is the default storage class for a global variable?
flashcards.back: Global variables have static storage duration by default and external linkage. They are stored in the data segment and exist for the entire program duration.

flashcards.front: Can static variables be initialized multiple times?
flashcards.back: No, static variables are initialized only once, at program startup. Subsequent assignments in functions execute but are not initializations.

flashcards.front: What happens if you apply '&' to a register variable?
flashcards.back: It is a compile-time error. The register storage class cannot use the address-of operator (&) because the variable may be stored in a CPU register which has no memory address.

flashcards.front: What is internal vs external linkage?
flashcards.back: Internal linkage (static at file scope): identifier visible only within that source file. External linkage (extern): identifier visible across multiple source files. Functions have external linkage by default.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between declaration and definition for extern?
flashcards.back: A declaration announces the type of a variable. A definition actually allocates storage. 'extern int x;' declares x; 'int x = 5;' defines and initializes x.
